coredata.dc:title: Interactions between carbon nanodots with human serum albumin and γ-globulins: The effects on the transportation function
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2015 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Xu, Zi-Qiang
Yang, Qi-Qi
Lan, Jia-Yi
Zhang, Jia-Qi
Peng, Wu
Jin, Jian-Cheng
Jiang, Feng-Lei
Liu, Yi
coredata.dc:description: Carbon nanodots (C-dots) have attracted great attention as a new class of luminescent nanomaterials due to their superior physical and chemical properties. In order to better understand the basic behavior of C-dots in biological systems, a series of photophysical measurements were applied to study the interactions of C-dots with human serum albumin (HSA) and γ-globulins. The fluorescence of proteins was quenched by the dynamic mechanism rather than the formation of a protein/C-dots complex. The apparent dissociation constants of the C-dots bound to HSA and γ-globulins were of the same order of magnitude. Furthermore, it is proven that C-dots showed little influence on the conformation of HSA and γ-globulins. In addition, Fourier transform infrared and fluorescence spectroscopic studies demonstrated that the interaction between C-dots and two kinds of serum proteins was driven by hydrophobic and van der waals forces. Since the bioavailability of drugs can be modulated by their interactions with proteins, the variations of binding constants of three drugs with HSA and γ-globulins in the presence of different concentrations of C-dots (0–84μmolL−1) have also been analyzed in this work, to reflect the effect of C-dots on the transportation function of HSA and γ-globulins.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon nanodots
Serum proteins
Protein–drug interaction

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescence quenching mechanism and binding constant
Force acting between C-dots and proteins
Effect of C-dots on the protein–drugs interaction.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization of C-dots. (a) Absorption and photoluminescence spectra of as-prepared C-dots. (b) HRTEM images of C-dots. (c) XPS spectra of C-dots. (d) FTIR spectra of C-dots.

Fluorescence emission spectra of C-dots/HSA (a) or C-dots/γ-globulins (b) complex in the presence of different concentrations of C-dots. Concentrations of C-dots were 0 to 168μmol/L at an interval of 16.8μmol/L. The inset corresponds to Stern–Volmer plot of the quenching of the fluorescence of HSA and γ-globulins by C-dots. [HSA]=2μmolL−1, [γ-globulins]=2μmolL−1.

Stern–Volmer plots for the C-dots/HSA (a) and C-dots/γ-globulins systems (b) at different temperature and at pH 7.4. [HSA]=2μmolL−1, [γ-globulins]= 2μmolL−1. UV–vis absorption spectra of C-dots, proteins and proteins/C-dots system. (c) for C-dots/HSA system, (d) for C-dots/γ-globulins system. [HSA]=2μmolL−1, [γ-globulins]=2μmolL−1, [C-dots]=168μmolL−1.

Fluorescence quenching properties of HSA (a) and γ-globulins (b) by C-dots. The red line represents a fit using Hill equation. CD spectra of HSA (c) and γ-globulins (d) in the presence of different concentrations of C-dots. [HSA]=2μmolL−1, [γ-globulins]=2μmolL−1.

The FTIR spectra of HSA (a) and γ-globulins (b) in the absence and presence of C-dots. [HSA]=10μmolL−1, [γ-globulins]=10μmolL−1, [C-dots]=140μmolL−1. The pH dependent Stern–Volmer plots for the C-dots/HSA (c) and C-dots/γ-globulins (d) systems. [HSA]=2μmolL−1, [γ-globulins]=2μmolL−1.
The effect of C-dots concentrations on the affinities of AsA (a), FA (b) and BH (c) for HSA and γ-globulins.
The affinities of three drugs for HSA and γ-globulins in the absence and presence of C-dots with different concentrations.

head.ce:title: Interactions between carbon nanodots with human serum albumin and γ-globulins: The effects on the transportation function

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Virology & Key Laboratory of Analytical Chemistry for Biology and Medicine (MOE), College of Chemistry and Molecular Sciences, Wuhan University, Wuhan 430072, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Virology & Key Laboratory of Analytical Chemistry for Biology and Medicine (MOE)
College of Chemistry and Molecular Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Hubei Collaborative Innovation Center for Advanced Organic Chemical Materials, Ministry-of-Education Key Laboratory for the Green Preparation and Application of Functional Materials, Hubei Province Key Laboratory of Industrial Biotechnology, Faculty of Materials Science & Engineering, Hubei University, Wuhan, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Hubei Collaborative Innovation Center for Advanced Organic Chemical Materials, Ministry-of-Education Key Laboratory for the Green Preparation and Application of Functional Materials
Hubei Province Key Laboratory of Industrial Biotechnology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon nanodots (C-dots) have attracted great attention as a new class of luminescent nanomaterials due to their superior physical and chemical properties. In order to better understand the basic behavior of C-dots in biological systems, a series of photophysical measurements were applied to study the interactions of C-dots with human serum albumin (HSA) and γ-globulins. The fluorescence of proteins was quenched by the dynamic mechanism rather than the formation of a protein/C-dots complex. The apparent dissociation constants of the C-dots bound to HSA and γ-globulins were of the same order of magnitude. Furthermore, it is proven that C-dots showed little influence on the conformation of HSA and γ-globulins. In addition, Fourier transform infrared and fluorescence spectroscopic studies demonstrated that the interaction between C-dots and two kinds of serum proteins was driven by hydrophobic and van der waals forces. Since the bioavailability of drugs can be modulated by their interactions with proteins, the variations of binding constants of three drugs with HSA and γ-globulins in the presence of different concentrations of C-dots (0–84μmolL−1) have also been analyzed in this work, to reflect the effect of C-dots on the transportation function of HSA and γ-globulins.

ce:section.ce:para: In comparison to their bulk counterparts, nanomaterials possess a relatively higher active surface to interact with environmental substances. When introducing into biological systems, nanomaterials can interact with various biological substances immediately, such as cell surface proteins, DNA, serum proteins as well as carbohydrates [1–3]. The adsorption of biomolecules, proteins in particular, on the surface of nanomaterials will form new biological entities (so called ‘protein corona’) in the biological milieu [4,5]. Consequently, the original interactions of nanomaterials with biological entities, such as cells, tissues and organs, will be replaced by the protein layer adsorbed on the surface of nanomaterials [6]. Therefore, understanding the interaction between nanomaterials and protein is essential for the development of functional and eco-friendly nanomaterials..

Carbon-based nanomaterials possess various fascinating properties derived from different allotropes and various microstructures. Fluorescent carbon-based nanomaterials have different forms, including nanodiamonds, fluorescent carbon nanotubes, fullerenes and a rising star, carbon dots (C-dots) [7]. The C-dots have emerged as candidates for a variety of applications, especially in the biomedical fields due to their superior photoluminescence (PL), photoelectronic properties and favorable biocompatibility. For example, C-dots have been applied as bioimaging probes, sensors, drug delivery systems and gene transfections [8–15]. However, before the sustained extensive research as well as practical biomedical applications, the thermodynamic and kinetic information of interactions between C-dots and proteins need to be elucidated [1,2].

Human blood plasma contains many proteins which perform various housekeeping function. In fact, plasma possesses the most complex human-derived proteome. It is estimated that up to 104 proteins are commonly present in plasma, while most of them are present in relative low abundances. The primary physiological function of serum proteins is reflected in regulating osmotic pressure and pH, aiding in the transportation of numerous endogenous and exogenous drugs or even toxicants as well as providing biomarker for disease diagnostics and treatment [3].

Recently, much attention has been paid to the interactions between nanomaterials and serum proteins. Lacerda et al. performed a series of photophysical measurements to quantify the interactions between common blood proteins and model gold nanomaterials with a wide range of diameters [3]. Shang et al. investigated the interactions of various fluorescent nanomaterials, including silver nanoclusters [16], gold nanoclusters [17,18] and CdSe/ZnS quantum dots (QDs) [19,20], with model serum protein, human serum albumin (HSA). In our previous studies, the binding properties of HSA with CdSe/ZnS QDs [21] and CdTe QDs with different coating ligands [22] or different sizes were also investigated systematically [23].

However, the interactions between C-dots with HSA and γ-globulins have rarely been reported. Based on a thorough understanding of these interactions, one can potentially predict and even control the behavior of C-dots at the nano–bio interface, which will greatly assist in their safe use in biomedical applications [6]. Thus, we present a detailed study on the interactions of C-dots with HSA and γ-globulins to better understand the basic behavior of C-dots in nanomedicine applications and under conditions of environmental exposure [3]. Detailed information about properties of HSA and γ-globulins has been given in supporting information. Moreover, drug-protein interactions are able to modulate the bio-availability of drugs, while the nanomaterials may influence the drug-protein interaction, and further affect the transportation function of HSA and γ-globulins as well as the metabolism and pharmacokinetics of drugs [24–26]. For instance, Xiao et al. studied the effects of CdTe G-QDs on affinities between flavonoids and HSA by fluorescence quenching method [24].

Herein, the effects of C-dots on affinities of two kinds of serum proteins with three drugs (two water-soluble vitamins, L-ascorbic acid (AsA) and folic acid (FA), as well as an important traditional herb, berberine hydrochloride (BH)) were studied to better understand the effect of C-dots on the transportation function of HSA and γ-globulins. Our work may attract people’s attention to the possible influence of nanomaterials on the function of proteins including the enzymes in vivo.

All chemicals used in this study were of analytical reagent grade. Sucrose, oleic acid, ascorbic acid, folic acid, berberine, sodium chloride (NaCl), potassium chloride (KCl), potassium phosphate dibasic (K2HPO4), potassium dihydrogen phosphate (KH2PO4) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. (China). HSA and γ-globulins were purchased from Sigma Co. (USA). All aqueous solutions were prepared with ultra pure water (18.2MΩcm−1, Millipore).

The C-dots were prepared by acidic oxidation methods based on previous reported synthetic methods [27]. The molar concentration was measured on the basis of the mean molecular weight (∼1194) determined by MALDI-TOF-MS (Fig. S1).

High resolution transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were recorded on a JEOL JEM-2100 (HR) electron microscope. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis was carried out with a KRATOS XSAM800 X-ray photoelectron spectrometer. Fourier Transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) spectra of C-dots were recorded by a Nicolet 360 FTIR spectrophotometer.

Fluorescence analysis was performed on LS-55 fluorophotometer (PerkinElmer, USA) in the ratio mode with temperature maintained by circulating bath. The excitation and emission wavelengths for proteins were 295 and 340nm, respectively. The width of the excitation and emission silt was set as 10.0, 15.0nm for HSA and 10.0, 12.0nm for γ-globulins. Each spectrum was the average of two scans. Titrations were performed by the trace syringes.
A UNICO 4802 UV–vis double-beam spectrophotometer was used to gain the absorption spectra. The absorption spectra were measured with a 1cm quartz cell between 200 and 600nm.

CD measurements were performed on a Circular Dichroism Photomultiplier (Applied Photophysics Limited, UK) at room temperature. The CD spectra of HSA and γ-globulins were recorded in the range of 260–200nm. The instrument was controlled by Jasco’s Spectra Manage™ software. Quartz cells with path lengths of 0.1cm were used and the scanning speed was set at 200nmmin−1. Appropriate buffer solutions, measured under the same experimental conditions, were taken as blanks and subtracted from the sample spectra. Each spectrum was the average of two scans.

FT-IR spectra were recorded at room temperature on VERTEX 70 spectrometer (Bruker, Germany) equipped with a zinc selenide (ZnSe) attenuated total reflection (ATR) accessory, a deuterated triglycine sulfate (DTGS) detector, and a KBr beam splitter. All spectra were taken via the ATR method with a resolution of 4cm−1.

C-dots were synthesized by carbonization of sucrose with oleic acid in one simple step [27]. The maximum emission wavelength of C-dots was centered at 521nm when excited by 410nm (Fig. 1 a). Typical UV–vis absorption spectra displayed two peaks centered at 220 and 280nm, which could be assigned to n → π* transition of CO bond and π → π* transition of aromatic sp2 bond. TEM images of C-dots demonstrated well dispersed and spherical nanomaterials (Fig. 1b). Fig. S2 shows the size distribution of C-dots. The statistical diameter of the C-dots was 2.4±0.6nm. The HRTEM images of the C-dots did not show any clear lattice fringes, indicating amorphous nature of the C-dots (Fig. 1b). This was further supported by XRD pattern which expressed a broad peak at around 18.5° (Fig. S3). The XPS spectra showed two peaks at 284.8 and 532.8eV which could be attributed to C1s and O1s (Fig. 1c). In the FTIR spectra (Fig. 1d), the absorption bands at 3404, 2928, 1664, 1414 and 1034cm−1 could be assigned to ν OH, ν CH, ν CO, δ CH and ν CO, respectively. All these above characterization indicated that C-dots consist of an amorphous core functionalized with oxygen related groups.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence quenching mechanism and binding constant
ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescence quenching efficiency and the quenching mechanism of protein by C-dots were studied by steady-state fluorescence techniques. The effect of C-dots on fluorescence intensity of protein was shown in Fig. 2 . Both fluorescence intensities of HSA and γ-globulins were obviously quenched by the addition of C-dots with no obvious shift of maximum emission wavelength (excitation wavelength was 295nm, 298K). The decrease of the intensity indicated that C-dots can bind to HSA and γ-globulins. In addition, the non-shift of emission wavelength indicated that C-dots have little influence on the microenvironment of tryptophan residue [28].

The mechanisms of quenching are usually classified as either dynamics quenching (collisional encounters) or static quenching (complex formation). These can be distinguished by different dependence on temperature and viscosity, or preferably by fluorescence lifetime measurements [29]. In general, for dynamic quenching, higher temperature results in larger diffusion coefficient and hence increased quenching efficiency. On the contrary, for static quenching, higher temperature typically results in the dissociation of weakly bound complexes and then decreased quenching efficiency. The fluorescence quenching can be described by the well-known Stern–Volmer (SV) equation [30] expressed as:
F
0
F
= 1 +
K
s v
[ C ]
where F 0 and F are fluorescence intensities in the absence and presence of the C-dots, [C] represents the concentration of C-dots. The quenching constant (K sv) is conventionally taken as a representative of the quenching efficiency. Insets of Fig. 2 show that at low concentration (from 0 to 42μmolL−1), both quenching results are in great agreement with the SV equation. However, at higher concentrations, results depart from the initial linearity. In order to avoid the inner filter effects [31], the experiment was carried within the linear part of SV equation (concentrations of C-dots vary from 0 to 42μmolL−1 at an interval of 4.2μmolL−1).

The effect of temperature on the K sv was also determined by SV equation. As shown in Fig. 3 a and b, both K sv are increased with increased temperature. These results demonstrate that the fluorescence quenching is initiated by the dynamic collision rather than the complex formation [29].

In order to further confirm that the possible quenching mechanism of HSA and γ-globulins by C-dots is dynamic collision, UV–vis absorption measurements were applied (Fig. 3c and d). The UV–vis absorption spectra of HSA (Fig. 3c) and γ-globulins (Fig. 3d) and the corresponding difference absorption spectra can be superposed with each other in the experimental error. Dynamics quenching only affects the excited states of the fluorophores but does not influence the absorption spectra [31]. Herein, this result further confirms the proposed quenching mechanism is dynamics quenching. The binding of C-dots to HSA or γ-globulins were further analyzed by fitting the fluorescence data to Hill equation [32],
Q
Q max
=
[ C ]
n
(
K D n
+
[ C ]
n
)
Q =(F 0 − F)/F0, where Q max is the saturation value of Q, K D is the apparent dissociation constant, and n represents the hill coefficient, which is generally regarded as a measure of association ‘cooperacivity’. For a positively cooperative reaction, n >1, meaning that once one protein molecule is bound to the nanomaterials, its affinity for the nanomaterials progressively increases in a superlinear fashion. For a negatively cooperative reaction, n <1, the binding strength of the protein to the nanomaterials becomes progressively weaker as further proteins adsorb. For a noncooperative association, where n =1 and where Q formally has the mathematical form of Langmuir adsorption equation the affinity of the proteins for nanomaterials does not depend on whether other protein molecules are already bound [3].

As shown in Fig. 4 a and b, an excellent agreement is achieved by fitting (F 0 − F)/F as a function of the C-dots concentration [C] with Hill equation, yielding K D(HSA) =5.4×10−5 molL−1, K D(γ-globulins) =8.9×10−5 molL−1. These values are much larger than the previously reported K D value of serum adsorption onto larger metal nanoparticles [3] or conventional semiconductor QDs [19,20]. It may be attributed to the much smaller sizes of C-dots then metal nanoparticles and conventional semiconductor QDs. And the surface of C-dots is functionalized with high content of various oxygen-related groups which are unfavorable for the interaction between C-dots and protein. Moreover, both n are larger than one which indicates that C-dots bind to HSA and γ-globulins in a positively cooperative manner.

CD spectra measurement, a sensitive and powerful analytical tool is used to investigate the influence of C-dots on the secondary structural of HSA and γ-globulins. Fig. 4c and d shows typical CD spectra of HSA and γ-globulins in the presence and absence of C-dots with the results expressed in terms of mean residue ellipticity (MRE). Consistent with the literature [3], the CD spectra of HSA exhibit two negative bands in the UV region at 210 and 220nm, while γ-globulins show a typical band at 218nm. The addition of C-dots showed no obvious change on the characteristic peptide CD bands of HSA or γ-globulins, which are sensitive to the change of secondary structure, indicating that C-dots do not induce any perturbations of protein fold.

FTIR spectroscopy is another valuable method to monitor changes in the secondary structure of proteins and their interactions with other molecules. FTIR spectra can reflect the change of three-dimensional structure of HSA or γ-globulins and the conformation of secondary structures for the complex of protein/C-dots [21]. In FTIR spectra (Fig. 5 a and b), the frequencies of bands due to amide I and amide II vibrations are sensitive to the change of secondary structure of proteins. The amide Ⅰ band occurs in the region 1600–1700cm−1 (mainly CO stretch) and amide Ⅱ peak occurs in the region 1500–1550cm−1 (CN stretch coupled with NH bending mode) [33]. As shown in FTIR spectra, the bands of amide I and amide II do not show any obvious shift. These results indicated that the secondary structure of HSA and γ-globulins has not been changed with the addition of C-dots. The results identified by the FTIR are in good agreement with CD spectra.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Force acting between C-dots and proteins
ce:section.ce:para: The types of biophysical forces involved in the interaction of drugs with protein (electrostatic, hydrophobic, hydrogen bonding and van der waals) are similar to those involved in the interaction of nanomaterial with proteins, accounting for the significant biological activity of nanomaterials [3].

No observed shift of amide I (∼1650cm−1) and amide II (∼1545cm−1) absorption peaks in FTIR spectra (Fig. 5a and b) indicates that both HSA and γ-globulins do not form hydrogen bonds with C-dots [33]. The electrostatic attraction may be the binding force between HSA or γ-globulins with C-dots. To verify the role of electrostatic force and take isoelectric points of proteins (4.7 for HSA [34] and 6.9 for γ-globulins [35]) into account, we investigate the effect of pH on C-dots/protein interaction (HSA: 4.0, 5.0, 7.0; γ-globulins: 6.0, 7.0, 9.0). SV plots for quenching of protein fluorescence by C-dots under different pHs were given in Fig. 5c and 5d. As shown in Fig. 5c and d, the solution pH has little influence on the K sv, especially for the γ-globulins. It is obvious that the electrostatic attraction may not be the main driving force for the interactions of HSA and γ-globulins with C-dots. Then pH dependent quenching studies as well as the FTIR spectra indicate that HSA and γ-globulins may interact with C-dots through hydrophobic and van der waals.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of C-dots on the protein–drugs interaction.
ce:section.ce:para: Then we have also studied the effect of C-dots on the affinities of drugs with HSA and γ-globulins. Herein, AsA, FA and BH (Fig. S4) are studied for the affinities for HSA or γ-globulins in the absence and presence of C-dots.

Firstly, we investigate the changes of binding constants between AsA and two kinds of proteins in the presence of C-dots. In biological systems, AsA (vitamin C) is a highly effective antioxidant acting to lessen oxidative, a substrate for ascorbate peroxidase, as well as an enzyme cofactor for the synthesis of biologically important molecules [36]. Since the quenching mechanism between two kinds of proteins and AsA is dynamic rather than static [37], double-logarithm equation was applied [38] to determine the binding constants in the presence of C-dots.
log
10
[
F
0
− F ]
F
=
log
10
K a
+
n log
10
[ C ]
where K a is the binding constant and n is the number of binding sites per protein. Fig. S5 shows the typical double-logarithm curves of AsA quenching the fluorescence of HSA and γ-globulins in the absence or presence of C-dots (84μmolL−1). Table 1 and Fig. 6 a show the effect of C-dots concentrations (2.1–84μmolL−1) on the affinities of AsA for HSA or γ-globulins. It is obvious that the binding constants of AsA for HSA or γ-globulins have the same order of magnitude in the absence or presence of C-dots with different concentrations, which means that the C-dots have limited influence on affinities of AsA with HSA and γ-globulins. For HSA, with increasing of C-dots concentrations, the binding constants of HSA with AsA slightly improved at first and then obviously decreased. But for γ-globulins, C-dots showed no obvious effect on the affinity of AsA for γ-globulins.

The influence of C-dots concentration on the affinities of FA with HSA or γ-globulins was then investigated. FA, which is known as vitamin B9, belongs to groups B vitamins. It has been reported [39] that FA has a high therapeutic effect on the treatment of malignant anemia. FA is essential to maintain the normal movement of an organism. Many biochemical and medical studies have confirmed that FA as a coenzyme is very important to the biosynthesis of purines, pyrimidines, nucleic acid, and protein and to the division and formation of cells [37].

As the quenching mechanism between two kinds of proteins and FA is static rather than dynamic [39], we applied modified SV equation [40] to determine the binding constants in the presence of C-dots.
F 0
F 0
− F
=
1
f a
K a
1
[ C ]
+
1
f a
where f a is the fraction of accessible fluorescence, Fig. S6 shows the typical modified SV curves of FA quenching the fluorescence of HSA and γ-globulins in the absence or presence of C-dots (84μmolL−1). Table 1 and Fig. 6b show the effect of C-dots concentrations (2.1–84μmolL−1) on the affinities of FA for HSA or γ-globulins. It is very clear that C-dots also show little effect on binding between FA and HSA as well as γ-globulins.

Finally, we have also studied the influence of C-dots concentration on the affinities of BH with HSA or γ-globulins. BH has been effectively used in the treatment of dysentery, diarrhea, stomatitis, throat infections, and hepatitis in folk medicine. In addition to its medicinal uses, BH is also used as a fluorescent probe of DNA and energized mitochondria in biochemical researches [28].

As the quenching mechanism between two kinds of proteins and BH is static [28], we applied modified SV equation to determine the binding constants in the presence of C-dots. Fig. S7 shows the typical modified SV curves of BH quenching the fluorescence of HSA and γ-globulins in the absence or presence of C-dots (84μmolL−1). Table 1 and Fig. 6c show the influence of C-dots concentrations (2.1–84μmolL−1) on the affinities of BH for HSA or γ-globulins. Interestingly, as shown in the Fig. 6c, the affinities of BH for HSA slightly decrease at the beginning and then improve obviously with the addition of C-dots (4.2–84μmolL−1). However, for γ-globulins, the binding constants did not show any changes when 2.1μmolL−1 C-dots were added, while they enhanced about three times with the concentrations of C-dots kept increasing.

Proteins, especially the serum proteins have the basic ability to selectively bind to small bioactive molecules. In many cases, the binding of first ligand to the protein can change the affinity of protein with second ligand, which is usually called cooperative binding phenomenon. This phenomenon play a special role in the physiological regulation of proteins function and can be either positive or negative, as mentioned above [24]. The cooperative binding effect may be attributed the changes of surface property or conformational of protein, induced by ligands and then further influence the interactions between the ligands.

The changes of binding constants between drugs and proteins in the presence of different amounts of C-dots may simply reflect the effect of C-dots on the transportation function of the HSA and γ-globulins. Our work shows that the part of the transportation function of HSA and γ-globulins can be affected by the C-dots in vivo. In addition, the influences of C-dots on the transportation capacity of different drugs by two kinds of proteins are various. The affinities between drugs and two kinds of proteins may be improved by C-dots while those between other drugs and two kinds of proteins may decrease.

In this work, a series of photophysical measurements were performed to quantify the interaction of C-dots with HSA and γ-globulins. The results demonstrated that the fluorescence quenching was initiated by the dynamic collisions. The two K D of C-dots with two HSA and γ-globulins were determined to be K D(HSA) =5.4×10−5 molL−1, K D(γ-globulins) =8.9×10−5 molL−1, respectively, which are of the same order of magnitude. The CD and FTIR spectra confirmed that C-dots show little influence on the conformation of HSA or γ-globulins. The C-dots interact with HSA and γ-globulins through hydrophobic and van der waals forces supported by FTIR and pH dependent quenching studies. The influence of the C-dots on the interaction of three drugs (AsA, FA, BH) with HSA and γ-globulins was also studied. For AsA, the binding constants for HSA first slightly improved and then obviously decreased while they were hardly changed with increasing concentrations of C-dots. However, the concentrations of C-dots showed no obvious effect on affinity of FA for HSA or γ-globulins. Interestingly, the affinities of BH for HSA or γ-globulins improved obviously with the increasing of C-dots concentrations. The variations of the binding affinities of drugs with HSA and γ-globulins reflect the effect of C-dots on the transportation function of the proteins. Our work is significant to explore the possible interference of nanomaterials on the function of proteins including enzymes in vivo.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge the financial support from Chinese 973 Program (Grant No. 2011CB933600), National Science Fund for Distinguished Young Scholars of China (Grant No. 21225313), National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 21303126, 21473125), Hubei Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 3014CFA003), Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (No. 2042014kf0287).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.jhazmat.2015.08.062.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following are Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Small fluorescent nanoparticles at the nano–bio interface
Interaction of gold nanoparticles with common human blood proteins
Understanding the nanoparticle–protein corona using methods to quantify exchange rates and affinities of proteins for nanoparticles
Proc. Natl. Acad. Sci. U. S. A.
Toward a molecular understanding of nanoparticle–protein interactions
Biomolecular coronas provide the biological identity of nanosized materials

Economical, green synthesis of fluorescent carbon nanoparticles and their use as probes for sensitive and selective detection of mercury(II) ions

Hydrothermal treatment of grass: a low-cost, green route to nitrogen-doped, carbon-rich, photoluminescent polymer nanodots as an effective fluorescent sensing platform for label-free detection of Cu(II) ions
Carbon nanodots: synthesis, properties and applications
Focusing on luminescent graphene quantum dots: current status and future perspectives
Carbon quantum dots for optical bioimaging
Functional surface engineering of C-dots for fluorescent biosensing and in vivo bioimaging
Recent advances in bioapplications of C-dots
Ultrasmall fluorescent silver nanoclusters: protein adsorption and its effects on cellular responses
Effect of protein adsorption on the fluorescence of ultrasmall gold nanoclusters
Nanoparticles interacting with proteins and cells: a systematic study of protein surface charge effects
A quantitative fluorescence study of protein monolayer formation on colloidal nanoparticles
Impact of protein modification on the protein corona on nanoparticles and nanoparticle–cell interactions
